name: Maize Lethal Necrosis (MLN)

symptoms.early: Yellowing from leaf tips inward, pale green to yellow streaking along leaf veins
symptoms.middle: Brown necrotic patches on leaves, starting at tips, spreading inward
symptoms.late: Complete plant chlorosis, premature death, rotting ears with poor grain fill
symptoms.diagnostic_feature: Yellowing always starts at LEAF TIPS and progresses INWARD toward midrib — distinguishes from nutrient deficiencies

cause: Co-infection of Maize Chlorotic Mottle Virus (MCMV) with Sugarcane Mosaic Virus (SCMV) or Wheat Streak Mosaic Virus
spread: Highly contagious. Wind, rain splash, insects, contaminated tools, infected seed

treatment.chemical: NO CURE. Remove and destroy all infected plants immediately.
steps: 1. Uproot infected plants carefully — bag and destroy by burning or deep burial (1m+)
2. Do NOT compost infected material
3. Control insect vectors immediately: apply Lambda-cyhalothrin or Imidacloprid
4. Clean all tools with bleach solution (10%) before moving to healthy sections
5. Plant tolerant/resistant varieties after a 2-month break
treatment.urgency: ACT TODAY — notify extension officer immediately

prevention: Plant MLN-tolerant varieties (SEEDCO SC403, H614D, WEMA MLN-tolerant lines)
Use certified disease-free seed from accredited dealers
Control thrip and aphid populations from emergence
Avoid movement of plant material from affected to clean fields
Rotate to non-host crops (beans, vegetables) for at least 1 season
reporting: MANDATORY — notifiable disease in Kenya, Tanzania, Uganda. Contact local Agriculture Extension Officer or Ministry of Agriculture.
distinguish_from_nitrogen_deficiency: MLN yellowing starts from LEAF TIPS (apex). Nitrogen deficiency starts from LOWER LEAVES on older plants and V-shapes up the midrib.

name: Fall Armyworm (FAW)

symptoms.early: 'Windowpane' feeding on young leaves — transparent patches where leaf tissue is eaten but leaf 'skin' remains
symptoms.middle: Ragged, irregular holes in leaves; caterpillar visible in whorl (curled young leaves) with dark frass (excrement)
symptoms.late: Destroyed whorls, dead heart symptom, ear damage in older plants
symptoms.diagnostic_feature: Look for: caterpillar has distinctive INVERTED Y on head capsule; 4 dots in square on second-to-last body segment

cause: Spodoptera frugiperda moth larvae. Moth is nocturnal, larvae active at night.
vectors: Wind-borne adult moths — can travel 100km per night
spread: Rapid. One female lays 1,500+ eggs. Three generations per season possible.

treatment.chemical: Emamectin benzoate (0.2% EC) — most effective. Or Chlorpyrifos, Spinetoram.
treatment.biological: Bacillus thuringiensis var. kurstaki (Bt) — effective on young (1st-3rd instar) caterpillars only
treatment.application: SPRAY INTO THE WHORL — caterpillars hide inside. Morning or evening application preferred.
steps: 1. Scout EVERY field every 3-4 days from emergence
2. Action threshold: 20%+ of plants with fresh damage
3. Mix emamectin benzoate at 20ml per 20L water; spray 5mL directly into whorl per plant
4. Wear full PPE: gloves, mask, goggles, long sleeves
5. Spray in the early morning or evening — avoid midday heat
6. Repeat after 7-10 days if damage continues
treatment.urgency: ACT THIS WEEK — populations double every few days

prevention: Push-pull intercropping: plant Desmodium between maize rows, Napier grass as border
Early planting to escape peak moth flight periods
Egg mass scouting from V1 stage — crush egg masses by hand
Pheromone traps for monitoring adult populations
Encourage natural enemies: birds, parasitic wasps, ground beetles
reporting: Report to extension officer — widespread monitoring helps track spread across regions
organic_options: Bt kurstaki — effective on small larvae only
Wood ash in whorl — deterrent effect, low cost

name: Bean Rust

symptoms.early: Small pale yellow spots on upper leaf surface; corresponding small tan/brown lesions on underside
symptoms.middle: Rust-colored (red-brown) powdery pustules on leaf undersides; pale yellow halos on upper surface
symptoms.late: Leaves turn brown and drop prematurely; severe defoliation; pod spots in heavy infections
symptoms.diagnostic_feature: Rust-colored pustules that release orange/brown powder when rubbed — the 'rust' color gives the disease its name

cause: Fungal pathogen Uromyces appendiculatus. Favoured by warm days, cool nights, high humidity.

treatment.chemical: Mancozeb (0.2%) or copper-based fungicide. Tebuconazole for severe infections.
steps: 1. Begin fungicide spray at first sign of rust pustules
2. Mix mancozeb at 40g per 20L water
3. Spray both upper and LOWER leaf surfaces thoroughly
4. Repeat every 10-14 days while symptoms persist
5. Remove and destroy heavily infected plant material
treatment.urgency: ACT THIS WEEK — rust spreads rapidly in humid conditions

prevention: Plant rust-resistant varieties where available
Ensure good plant spacing (30cm apart) for airflow
Avoid overhead irrigation — wet leaves promote spore germination
Remove crop debris after harvest — destroys overwintering spores
Rotate with non-legume crops for 1-2 seasons
cost_estimate_kenya: Mancozeb 200g pack: KES 80-120. Treat 0.25 acre per pack.

name: Nitrogen Deficiency
severity: HIGH — causes 20-40% yield loss if untreated
regions: Global — most common on sandy or degraded soils

symptoms.early: Lower (older) leaves turn pale green or yellow-green
symptoms.middle: Characteristic 'V' shape yellowing starting from leaf tip, progressing along midrib toward the stalk
symptoms.late: Upper leaves turn yellow; stunted plant growth; thin, spindly stalks; poor grain filling
symptoms.diagnostic_feature: STARTS ON LOWER/OLDER LEAVES and moves upward — opposite of MLN. The V-shaped yellowing along the midrib from leaf tip is the classic signature.

cause: Insufficient nitrogen in soil. Caused by: insufficient fertiliser, waterlogged soils reducing N uptake, sandy soils with rapid leaching, high soil pH (above 7.5) blocking uptake.

treatment.immediate: Top-dress with CAN (Calcium Ammonium Nitrate, 26% N) as soon as possible
steps: 1. Apply CAN at 50kg per acre (one bag) when soil is MOIST — never on dry soil
2. Apply in a ring around the plant 10cm from the stalk — not directly touching the stem
3. Water if possible after application
4. Organic option: 20L of diluted cattle urine (1:10 with water) applied around root zone
5. Results visible in 7-10 days
treatment.urgency: ACT THIS WEEK — yield loss accumulates daily during vegetative growth

prevention: Apply basal fertiliser (DAP) at planting
Top-dress with CAN at V4-V6 stage (knee-high)
Incorporate organic matter/manure before planting
Maintain soil pH 5.5-6.5 for optimal nitrogen uptake
Avoid waterlogging — improves root nitrogen absorption
distinguish_from_mln: Nitrogen deficiency starts from OLD leaves (bottom of plant) and V-shapes up the midrib from tip. MLN starts from LEAF TIPS and streaks inward. Different field pattern too: N deficiency is uniform across field; MLN appears in scattered patches.
cost_estimate_kenya: 1 bag CAN (50kg): KES 3,800-4,400. Enough for 1 acre top-dressing.
